sections.title: Phrasal verbs
items: **do up** — button/zip up a piece of clothing
**put on** — start wearing (a piece of clothing)
**try on** — put on (a piece of clothing) to see how it looks and if it fits

exercises.title: Topic vocabulary (gap fill)
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences with words from the box.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences with words from the box.

items.sentence: The castle contains ...... paintings from the Middle Ages.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is very old, so the correct answer is "ancient".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: My favourite T-shirt is made from pure ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is natural fabric, so the correct answer is "cotton".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: They want to ...... a new website for the company.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Dad needs to ...... the broken kitchen chair.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is repair, so the correct answer is "fix".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

exercises.title: Circle the correct word
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct option in each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct option in each sentence.

items.originalSentence: These jeans are too **tight / striped** for me now.
items.completedSentence: These jeans are too tight for me now.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "tight" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

wrongOptions.striped: "striped" does not match the present continuous time clue in this sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.striped: "striped" doesn't fit here → "tight" does, because does not match the present continuous time clue in this sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: She put the magazines in a
items.originalSentence: She put the magazines in a **pile / shape** beside the sofa.
items.completedSentence: She put the magazines in a pile beside the sofa.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "pile" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "pile" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "shape" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.shape: "shape" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("beside the sofa.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.shape: "shape" doesn't fit here → "pile" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("beside the sofa.").

items.sentenceBefore: After polishing it, the stone became very
items.originalSentence: After polishing it, the stone became very **rough / smooth**.
items.completedSentence: After polishing it, the stone became very smooth .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "smooth" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "smooth" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "rough" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.rough: "rough" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.rough: "rough" doesn't fit here → "smooth" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

items.originalSentence: Your shoes don't **stretch / match** your jacket.
items.completedSentence: Your shoes don't match your jacket.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "match" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "match" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "stretch" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.stretch: "stretch" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("your jacket.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.stretch: "stretch" doesn't fit here → "match" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("your jacket.").

items.sentenceBefore: Did you ...... the crack in the window?
items.originalSentence: Did you ...... the crack in the window? **notice / suit**
items.completedSentence: Did you ...... the crack in the window? notice

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "notice" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "notice" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "suit" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.suit: "suit" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.suit: "suit" doesn't fit here → "notice" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: This is the best ...... for painting walls.
items.originalSentence: This is the best ...... for painting walls. **tool / style**
items.completedSentence: This is the best ...... for painting walls. tool

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "tool" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "tool" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "style" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.style: "style" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.style: "style" doesn't fit here → "tool" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

exercises.title: Phrasal verbs (complete the gaps)
exercises.instructions: Write the correct phrasal verb in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write the correct phrasal verb in each gap.

items.sentence: You should ...... a jumper because it's getting colder.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is wear, so the correct answer is "put on".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "put on", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Please ...... your hat when you enter the classroom.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is remove, so the correct answer is "take off".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "take off", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: I'd like to ...... these trainers before buying them.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is test before buying, so the correct answer is "try on".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "try on", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Be careful not to ...... any important information.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is omit, so the correct answer is "leave out".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "leave out", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Can you ...... your coat properly? It's still open.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is fasten, so the correct answer is "do up".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "do up", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Could you ...... my glass with juice?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is make full, so the correct answer is "fill up".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "fill up", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "fill up" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

items.sentence: He always ...... a black jacket at concerts.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is wearing, so the correct answer is "has on".

wrongOptions.on: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "has on", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The tailor had to ...... the damaged fabric.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is remove by cutting, so the correct answer is "cut off".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "cut off", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

exercises.title: Prepositional phrases

exercises.title: Word patterns
exercises.instructions: Write one preposition in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one preposition in each gap.

items.sentence: Everyone was amazed ...... her artistic talent.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Amazed at her talent.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: Tom was disappointed ...... the final result.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Disappointed with the result.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence: Are you familiar ...... this type of material?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Familiar with this material.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: She was involved ...... designing the new theatre.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Involved in designing.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence: This painting is similar ...... one I saw in Paris.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Similar to one in Paris.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The company changed the warehouse ...... offices.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: This smell reminds me ...... my grandmother's kitchen.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Reminds me of her kitchen.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Please remove the sticker ...... the box.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Remove from the box.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: The guide described the castle ...... impressive and unique.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Describe as impressive.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "as" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "The guide described the castle ...... impressive and unique.".

exercises.title: Word formation
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct form of the word in capitals.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with the correct form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: The famous ...... painted several murals in the city. (ART)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is person from ART, so the correct answer is "artist".

wrongOptions.art: The root ART cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "artist".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of ART that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: ART → artist: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "artist" is the word-formation answer from the root ART in this context.

items.sentence: This phone case is nearly ...... . (BREAK)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is negative from BREAK, so the correct answer is "unbreakable".

wrongOptions.break: The root BREAK cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "unbreakable".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of BREAK that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: BREAK → unbreakable: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "unbreakable" is the word-formation answer from the root BREAK in this context.

items.sentence: Beethoven was an incredible ...... . (COMPOSE)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is person from COMPOSE, so the correct answer is "composer".

wrongOptions.compose: The root COMPOSE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "composer".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of COMPOSE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: COMPOSE → composer: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "composer" is the word-formation answer from the root COMPOSE in this context.

items.sentence: We visited an ...... about modern architecture. (EXHIBIT)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from EXHIBIT, so the correct answer is "exhibition".

wrongOptions.exhibit: The root EXHIBIT cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "exhibition".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of EXHIBIT that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: EXHIBIT → exhibition: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "exhibition" is the word-formation answer from the root EXHIBIT in this context.

items.sentence: Education can give people greater ...... . (FREE)

items.sentence: Only a ...... of guests stayed until midnight. (HAND)

wrongOptions.hand: The root HAND cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "handful".

exercises.title: G: Gap fill
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with a word or phrase from the box.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with a word or phrase from the box.

items.sentence: These boots don't ...... me because they are too small.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is fit well, so the correct answer is "suit".

explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.

items.sentence: Can you ...... the map so it fits in your pocket?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is make smaller, so the correct answer is "fold".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
